title: Lesson 400
transcript: All right, we just talked about role-based access, and here's just a quick bit of terminology you need to know, the difference between roles and groups. So, what are roles? Roles are what we just talked about, right? A role is where you, the way role-based access works is you create a role, and it's the role that defines what the access is. It's function centric, right? So you create a role for the payroll manager level one, something like that. So a role is a set of permissions that correspond to a job function. And this is slightly and subtly different than groups. Groups are just collections of users. So you put a bunch of users together, and then you say, OK, we'll give these users some access. So this is subtle, but the difference between roles and groups is that a role is very specific. And I think it's summarized well down here. Roles are function centric. So a role says payroll manager level one gets this access. Payroll manager level two gets this access. So you create roles and the roles define the access. And an employee can have one or more different roles assigned to them. Groups is where you group different users together. And these user groups get some sort of access. But just remember this really subtle difference. Roles are function centric, whereas groups are user centric. Probably not super important though for the exam, but just in case, that's the difference between roles and groups.
